# Now just run Aider without flags - config is loaded automatically
aider src/app.py
```

### Example 4

```python
# Example 4: View and Undo Commits
# In an active Aider session, view recent commit history"/log --oneline"

# Output might look like:
# a1b2c3d fixed null check in user validation (2 minutes ago)
# e4f5g6h added password reset functionality (15 minutes ago)
# i7j8k9l initial project setup (1 hour ago)

# Undo the last commit if it needs revision"/undo"

# This reverts the commit but keeps your file changes
# You can then edit and recommit with a better message
```

### Example 5

```python
# Example 5: Rollback with Git Reset
# After using /undo in Aider, you can use git commands directly
# to perform more advanced rollback operations

# Soft reset: Keeps changes staged
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# Mixed reset: Keeps changes unstaged (default)
git reset HEAD~1

# Hard reset: Completely removes changes (BE CAREFUL!)
git reset --hard HEAD~1

# In Aider, you can also use:
# /undo --hard  # To completely remove the last commit and changes
```

### Example 6

```python
# Example 6: Complete Workflow Example
# Terminal session showing auto-commit workflow

# Step 1: Start Aider with auto-commit
aider --auto-commit src/models/user.py

# Step 2: Aider session (AI makes an edit)
# You: "Add a method to validate email format"
# Aider: [makes changes to user.py]
# Aider: "I've added an email validation method. Changes auto-committed."

# Step 3: View the commit that was created
# In terminal, outside Aider:
git log -1 --stat
# commit a1b2c3d...
# Add email validation method in user.py
#
#     src/models/user.py | 15 ++---------------
#     1 file changed, 15 insertions(+)

# Step 4: Continue working, each change is auto-committed
aider> "Now add a method to hash passwords"
# Aider: [makes changes]
# Aider: "Added password hashing method. Auto-committed as commit b2c3d4e..."

# Step 5: If something goes wrong, rollback
aider> /undo
# Aider: "Reverted last commit. Your changes are still in the files."
```



## Common Mistakes

- Forgetting to enable auto-commit: Running Aider without --auto-commit and losing changes if the session crashes or disconnects
- Not reviewing generated commit messages: Assuming all auto-generated messages are perfect without checking them first
- Working on shared branches with auto-commit: Creating messy commit history on main/master branches that affects the whole team
- Ignoring .gitignore: Allowing auto-commit to track temporary files, build artifacts, or sensitive configuration files
- Using hard reset without understanding: Running git reset --hard after /undo and permanently losing code changes
- Mixing unrelated changes: Asking Aider to make unrelated edits in one session, resulting in commits that don't represent logical units
- Not pulling before starting: Creating divergent history by not syncing with remote before starting an auto-commit session
- Committing generated files automatically: Including compiled binaries or generated documentation that should be gitignored


## Best Practices

- Enable auto-commit as your default: Add auto-commit to your permanent Aider configuration so it's always on, protecting your work from loss
- Review commit history regularly: Use /log to understand the commit pattern and ensure messages are meaningful and accurate
- Use feature branches for major work: Create a new branch with git checkout -b feature-name before starting Aider sessions, then merge when complete
- Configure custom commit patterns: Set up a commit-msg-format that matches your team's conventions for consistent history
- Push commits frequently: Don't let local commits accumulate; push regularly to avoid losing work if your machine fails
- Use /undo sparingly and carefully: Only use when you need to reword or reorganize commits, and always verify your changes after
- Exclude generated files: Add patterns to .aiderignore for build outputs, dependencies, and other files that shouldn't be committed
- Combine auto-commit with meaningful conversations: Give Aider context about why you're making changes so it generates more accurate commit messages

# Example 1: Basic Auto-Commit Setup
# Start Aider with auto-commit enabled for a Python project
aider --auto-commit --file src/main.py

# This command starts Aider and enables automatic commits
# Every time the AI makes edits to files, a commit will be created
# with a descriptive message generated by Aider

# Example 2: Custom Commit Message Pattern
# Start Aider with a custom commit message format
aider --auto-commit \
  --commit-msg-format "{action}: {description} ({filename})"

# After editing, you might see commits like:
# "modified: Fix authentication bug in login.py"
# "added: Implement user profile feature (profile.py)"
# "removed: Delete deprecated helper function (utils.py)"

# Example 3: Configuration File Setup
# Create .aider.conf.yml in your project root
# This sets up permanent auto-commit behavior
cat > .aider.conf.yml << 'EOF'
# Enable automatic commits after each edit
auto-commit: true

# Custom message format for better clarity
commit-msg-format: "{action}: {description} in {filename}"

# Files to exclude from auto-commit
exclude:
  - "*.min.js"
  - "vendor/**"
  - "node_modules/**"
EOF

sub_nodes.title: Branching and Merging
sub_nodes.description: Using AI to create feature branches, propose merge strategies, and resolve conflicts.
learning_points: Prompt Aider to suggest merge steps
Resolve conflicts by feeding conflict markers to Aider

details.detailed_content:
# Branching and Merging with Aider: AI-Assisted Version Control

## Concept Explanation

### Why Branching Matters in Modern Development

Branching is the foundation of collaborative software development. It allows multiple developers to work on different features, bug fixes, or experiments simultaneously without interfering with each other's work. When done correctly, branches create isolated environments where code can evolve independently before being integrated back into the main codebase.

### The AI Integration Advantage

Traditional branching workflows require developers to manually determine merge strategies, understand complex conflict scenarios, and carefully edit conflicting code sections. Aider transforms this experience by providing intelligent assistance throughout the branch lifecycle—from creation to merge completion. The AI can analyze code differences, suggest optimal merge approaches, and help resolve conflicts by understanding the intent behind both versions of the code.

### Why This Matters for Intermediate Developers

At the intermediate level, developers typically understand basic Git commands but may struggle with complex merge scenarios, especially in teams with frequent concurrent development. AI-assisted branching and merging bridges this gap by providing contextual guidance that helps developers learn best practices while maintaining productivity.

---

## Core Mechanics

### 1. Creating Feature Branches with AI Assistance

The journey begins with creating a well-structured feature branch. Aider can assist in choosing appropriate branch names and even suggest based on your workflow conventions. The fundamental command remains `git checkout -b <branch-name>`, but Aider can enhance this by analyzing your existing branches and project patterns.

When creating a branch, consider the following AI-assisted aspects:
- **Branch naming conventions**: Aider can learn your project's naming patterns
- **Base branch selection**: AI can suggest the most appropriate base branch based on dependency analysis
- **Scope verification**: Ensure the branch name reflects a single, focused change

### 2. Merge Strategy Suggestions

One of Aider's most valuable features is its ability to propose merge strategies. Rather than blindly performing `git merge`, you can consult Aider to understand the implications of different merge approaches:

- **Fast-forward merges**: When the main branch hasn't advanced, Aider can suggest a clean fast-forward
- **Recursive merges**: When parallel development has occurred, AI can analyze the commit history and suggest appropriate merge commit messages
- **Rebase vs. merge decisions**: Aider can evaluate your branch history and recommend whether rebase would create a cleaner history

### 3. Conflict Resolution with AI

Merge conflicts are inevitable in collaborative development. When Git cannot automatically resolve differences, Aider becomes invaluable. Instead of manually parsing conflict markers and making educated guesses about the correct resolution, you can:

1. Feed the conflict context to Aider
2. Receive explanations of why conflicts exist
3. Get suggestions for optimal resolutions based on understanding both code paths
4. Iterate on resolutions with natural language refinement

### 4. Branch Comparison with /diff

Before merging, understanding what changed is crucial. The `/diff` command in Aider provides intelligent comparison of branches, highlighting:
- Functional changes rather than just line differences
- Potential integration issues
- Areas that might need additional testing

---

## Workflow Integration

### Typical AI-Assisted Branching Workflow

1. **Pull latest changes** from the main branch
2. **Create a feature branch** with `git checkout -b feature/my-new-feature`
3. **Develop and commit** your changes
4. **Request merge strategy** from Aider before initiating merge
5. **Execute merge** and handle any conflicts with AI assistance
6. **Verify the result** using `/diff` and testing
7. **Clean up** the feature branch after successful merge

### Handling Long-Running Branches

When branches span multiple days or weeks, AI assistance becomes even more valuable:
- Regular sync with main branch prevents accumulation of conflicts
- Aider can track which conflicts were previously seen and how they were resolved
- Historical context helps AI provide better suggestions for re-integration

---

## Advanced Considerations

### Merge Conflict Types

Understanding the types of conflicts helps you work more effectively with Aider:

- **Text conflicts**: Direct text differences in the same location
- **Structural conflicts**: Changes affecting the same function or class architecture
- **Semantic conflicts**: Logically incompatible changes that appear valid independently

Aider excels particularly at semantic conflict resolution by understanding code intent.

### Handling Binary and Unmergeable Files

Not all conflicts can be resolved through code editing. Aider can guide you on:
- Choosing between conflicting binary assets
- Managing localization file conflicts
- Handling configuration merge conflicts

---

## Troubleshooting Branching Issues

### Detached HEAD States

If you accidentally enter detached HEAD state, Aider can help you recover by:
- Explaining the current state clearly
- Suggesting the correct branch to checkout
- Helping recover uncommitted work if any exists

### Lost Commits

In complex merge scenarios, commits might appear to be lost. Aider can help:
- Navigate reflog to find seemingly lost commits
- Explain the commit graph structure
- Suggest safe recovery strategies

### Branch Cleanup Strategies

Regular branch maintenance is essential. Aider can assist by:
- Identifying merged branches that can be deleted
- Highlighting stale branches not updated recently
- Suggesting branch organization improvements


## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
# Example 1: Creating a Feature Branch and Getting Merge Strategy Suggestions
# This example demonstrates the full workflow from branch creation to merge

# Step 1: Ensure you're on the main branch with latest changes
git checkout main
git pull origin main

# Step 2: Create a new feature branch
git checkout -b feature/user-authentication

# Step 3: Make your changes (example: creating an auth module)
# ... implement your feature code here ...
git add .
git commit -m "Add user authentication module"

# Step 4: Before merging, ask Aider for merge strategy suggestions
# In Aider, you would run:
# /merge-strategy main
# This asks Aider to analyze potential merge issues and suggest a strategy

# Step 5: If no conflicts, perform the merge
git checkout main
git merge feature/user-authentication

# Step 6: Clean up the feature branch after successful merge
git branch -d feature/user-authentication

# Alternative: Using Aider's merge assistance for complex scenarios
# First, switch to the branch you want to merge into
git checkout main
# Then ask Aider: "What is the best way to merge feature/user-authentication?"
# Aider will analyze the branch and provide specific recommendations
```

### Example 2

```python
# Example 2: Resolving Merge Conflicts with Aider
# This example shows how to handle a merge conflict using AI assistance

# Step 1: Attempt to merge a branch that has conflicts
git checkout main
git merge feature/payment-processing

# Step 2: Git will report conflicts - the output looks like:
# Auto-merging src/payment.js
# CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in src/payment.js
# Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.

# Step 3: View the conflicted file to see conflict markers
git diff  # Shows the conflict state

# Step 4: Feed the conflict to Aider for intelligent resolution
# Copy the content of the conflicted file (including markers) and paste to Aider
# Ask: "Please resolve these merge conflicts in src/payment.js"
# Aider will analyze both versions and suggest an optimal resolution

# Step 5: After Aider provides the resolution, apply it manually:
# Edit src/payment.js to contain the resolved code (remove <<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>> markers)

# Step 6: Stage the resolved file
git add src/payment.js

# Step 7: Complete the merge commit
git commit -m "Merge feature/payment-processing with conflict resolution"

# Step 8: Verify the merge result
git log --graph --oneline -10
# Also run: /diff HEAD~1 to see what changed in this merge
```

### Example 3

```python
# Example 3: Comparing Branches Before Merging
# This example demonstrates using /diff to understand changes before merging

# Scenario: You want to review feature branch changes before merging to main

# Step 1: Checkout the target branch (where you want to merge into)
git checkout main

# Step 2: Use Aider's /diff command to compare branches
# In Aider, run: /diff feature/new-feature
# This shows all differences between current branch and feature/new-feature

# Step 3: Aider will provide an intelligent comparison highlighting:
# - Files that changed
# - The nature of changes (additions, modifications, deletions)
# - Potential conflicts or integration issues
# - Areas that might need attention

# Step 4: Request specific analysis if needed
# Ask Aider: "Are there any breaking changes in this diff?"
# Aider will analyze the changes for potential issues

# Step 5: If everything looks good, proceed with merge
git merge feature/new-feature

# Step 6: After merging, compare with the parent to verify
git show-branch feature/new-feature main  # Shows relationship between branches

# Additional tip: Compare specific files across branches
git diff main..feature/new-feature -- src/specific-file.js
# Ask Aider: "Review this diff for src/specific-file.js"

# Cleanup after successful verification
git branch -d feature/new-feature
```



## Common Mistakes

- Not pulling the latest changes from the main branch before creating a new feature branch, leading to outdated base and increased merge conflicts later
- Ignoring merge conflicts or attempting to resolve them without understanding both code paths, resulting in incorrect resolutions that break functionality
- Accepting AI-generated merge resolutions without reviewing the changes, as AI suggestions may not always consider project-specific requirements or business logic
- Creating branches with generic or unclear names like 'test' or 'fix', making it difficult to track branch purpose and history
- Forgetting to run tests after AI-assisted conflict resolution, as automated resolution might introduce subtle bugs that manual testing would catch
- Leaving conflict markers unfinalized and committing with unresolved markers, which breaks the code for the entire team
- Deleting feature branches immediately after merging without verifying the merge succeeded, losing the ability to revert if issues are discovered
- Using --force during merge operations when not absolutely necessary, potentially losing work or creating inconsistent repository history
- Not using /diff before merging to understand what changes will be integrated, leading to surprises and unanticipated side effects


## Best Practices

- Always pull the latest changes from the main branch before creating a new feature branch to minimize future integration challenges
- Keep branches small and short-lived (ideally less than a few days), reducing the scope for conflicts and making reviews more manageable
- Use descriptive branch naming conventions like feature/user-auth, bugfix/login-crash, or chore/update-dependencies to communicate intent clearly
- Leverage Aider's /diff command before every merge to review all changes and catch potential issues early in the process
- When resolving conflicts with AI assistance, always review the proposed resolution in context before accepting it, considering project architecture and coding standards
- Run comprehensive tests (unit, integration, and if applicable, end-to-end) after every merge, especially those involving conflict resolution
- Delete feature branches after successful merges to keep the repository clean, but verify the merge completed correctly first
- Communicate with team members when working on branches that might affect shared code or dependencies, as AI can't fully understand team dynamics
- Use merge commits (rather than squashing or rebasing) when merges involve conflict resolution, as this preserves the accurate history of integration challenges
- Document unusual merge resolutions in commit messages so future maintainers understand why specific decisions were made during conflict resolution

git branch -d feature/new-feature
```



## Common Mistakes

- Not pulling the latest changes from the main branch before creating a new feature branch, leading to outdated base and increased merge conflicts later
- Ignoring merge conflicts or attempting to resolve them without understanding both code paths, resulting in incorrect resolutions that break functionality
- Accepting AI-generated merge resolutions without reviewing the changes, as AI suggestions may not always consider project-specific requirements or business logic
- Creating branches with generic or unclear names like 'test' or 'fix', making it difficult to track branch purpose and history
- Forgetting to run tests after AI-assisted conflict resolution, as automated resolution might introduce subtle bugs that manual testing would catch
- Leaving conflict markers unfinalized and committing with unresolved markers, which breaks the code for the entire team
- Deleting feature branches immediately after merging without verifying the merge succeeded, losing the ability to revert if issues are discovered
- Using --force during merge operations when not absolutely necessary, potentially losing work or creating inconsistent repository history
- Not using /diff before merging to understand what changes will be integrated, leading to surprises and unanticipated side effects


## Best Practices

- Always pull the latest changes from the main branch before creating a new feature branch to minimize future integration challenges
- Keep branches small and short-lived (ideally less than a few days), reducing the scope for conflicts and making reviews more manageable
- Use descriptive branch naming conventions like feature/user-auth, bugfix/login-crash, or chore/update-dependencies to communicate intent clearly
- Leverage Aider's /diff command before every merge to review all changes and catch potential issues early in the process
- When resolving conflicts with AI assistance, always review the proposed resolution in context before accepting it, considering project architecture and coding standards
- Run comprehensive tests (unit, integration, and if applicable, end-to-end) after every merge, especially those involving conflict resolution
- Delete feature branches after successful merges to keep the repository clean, but verify the merge completed correctly first
- Communicate with team members when working on branches that might affect shared code or dependencies, as AI can't fully understand team dynamics
- Use merge commits (rather than squashing or rebasing) when merges involve conflict resolution, as this preserves the accurate history of integration challenges
- Document unusual merge resolutions in commit messages so future maintainers understand why specific decisions were made during conflict resolution

# Example 1: Creating a Feature Branch and Getting Merge Strategy Suggestions
# This example demonstrates the full workflow from branch creation to merge

# Step 1: Ensure you're on the main branch with latest changes
git checkout main
git pull origin main

# Step 2: Create a new feature branch
git checkout -b feature/user-authentication

# Step 3: Make your changes (example: creating an auth module)
# ... implement your feature code here ...
git add .
git commit -m "Add user authentication module"

# Step 4: Before merging, ask Aider for merge strategy suggestions
# In Aider, you would run:
# /merge-strategy main
# This asks Aider to analyze potential merge issues and suggest a strategy

# Step 5: If no conflicts, perform the merge
git checkout main
git merge feature/user-authentication

# Step 6: Clean up the feature branch after successful merge
git branch -d feature/user-authentication

# Alternative: Using Aider's merge assistance for complex scenarios
# First, switch to the branch you want to merge into
git checkout main
# Then ask Aider: "What is the best way to merge feature/user-authentication?"
# Aider will analyze the branch and provide specific recommendations

# Example 2: Resolving Merge Conflicts with Aider
# This example shows how to handle a merge conflict using AI assistance

# Step 1: Attempt to merge a branch that has conflicts
git checkout main
git merge feature/payment-processing

# Step 2: Git will report conflicts - the output looks like:
# Auto-merging src/payment.js
# CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in src/payment.js
# Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.

# Step 3: View the conflicted file to see conflict markers
git diff  # Shows the conflict state

# Step 4: Feed the conflict to Aider for intelligent resolution
# Copy the content of the conflicted file (including markers) and paste to Aider
# Ask: "Please resolve these merge conflicts in src/payment.js"
# Aider will analyze both versions and suggest an optimal resolution

# Step 5: After Aider provides the resolution, apply it manually:
# Edit src/payment.js to contain the resolved code (remove <<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>> markers)

# Step 6: Stage the resolved file
git add src/payment.js

# Step 7: Complete the merge commit
git commit -m "Merge feature/payment-processing with conflict resolution"

# Step 8: Verify the merge result
git log --graph --oneline -10
# Also run: /diff HEAD~1 to see what changed in this merge

# Example 3: Comparing Branches Before Merging
# This example demonstrates using /diff to understand changes before merging

# Scenario: You want to review feature branch changes before merging to main

# Step 1: Checkout the target branch (where you want to merge into)
git checkout main

# Step 2: Use Aider's /diff command to compare branches
# In Aider, run: /diff feature/new-feature
# This shows all differences between current branch and feature/new-feature

# Step 3: Aider will provide an intelligent comparison highlighting:
# - Files that changed
# - The nature of changes (additions, modifications, deletions)
# - Potential conflicts or integration issues
# - Areas that might need attention

# Step 4: Request specific analysis if needed
# Ask Aider: "Are there any breaking changes in this diff?"
# Aider will analyze the changes for potential issues

# Step 5: If everything looks good, proceed with merge
git merge feature/new-feature

# Step 6: After merging, compare with the parent to verify
git show-branch feature/new-feature main  # Shows relationship between branches

# Additional tip: Compare specific files across branches
git diff main..feature/new-feature -- src/specific-file.js
# Ask Aider: "Review this diff for src/specific-file.js"

# Cleanup after successful verification
git branch -d feature/new-feature

details.detailed_content:
## Understanding Linting, Testing, and CI Integration in Aider

### Why This Knowledge Point Matters

In modern software development, maintaining code quality is not optional—it's essential. When collaborating with AI coding assistants like Aider, the integration of linting, testing, and CI pipelines becomes the backbone of reliable code delivery. Without these safeguards, AI-suggested changes can introduce subtle bugs, violate coding standards, or break existing functionality.

**The Core Problem It Solves:** Aider is powerful at generating and modifying code, but it doesn't inherently know your project's coding standards or validate that changes don't break existing behavior. By integrating linting and testing directly into your Aider workflow, you create a feedback loop that ensures AI-assisted changes meet quality standards before they ever reach your teammates or production.

**The Real-World Impact:** Teams using Aider without proper linting and testing integration often discover issues late in the development cycle, leading to hotfixes, debugging sessions, and frustrated developers. Conversely, teams that master this integration workflow ship cleaner code faster, with fewer regressions and more consistent codebase quality.

### Core Mechanics: How It Works

#### Running Linters from Aider

Aider provides the `/lint` command to run code quality tools directly within your conversation. This command invokes configured linters against your codebase and presents the results in a format you can immediately act on.

**The Workflow:**
1. Aider detects that you're working on code quality issues
2. You invoke `/lint <tool>` to run a specific linter
3. Aider parses the output and explains issues in natural language
4. You can ask Aider to fix the issues, which it does while maintaining code functionality


Linters catch issues like unused imports, variable naming violations, formatting inconsistencies, and potential bugs before they become problems. Popular choices include ESLint for JavaScript, Pylint/Flake8 for Python, and RuboCop for Ruby.

#### Executing Test Suites

The `/test` command runs your project's test suite directly from within Aider. This is crucial because it validates that AI-suggested changes don't break existing functionality.

**The Workflow:**
1. After making changes, invoke `/test pytest` (or your test framework)
2. Aider captures test results including pass/fail status and output
3. For failing tests, Aider analyzes the failure and suggests fixes
4. You can iterate between fixing and retesting until tests pass

This integration means you're never guessing whether a change works—you have concrete evidence from your test suite.

#### Interpreting AI Suggestions for Failing Tests

When tests fail, Aider's value intensifies. Instead of manually debugging, you can:

1. **Ask for diagnosis:** "Why is this test failing?"
2. **Request analysis:** Aider examines the test output, the test code, and your changes to identify root causes
3. **Get fixes:** Ask Aider to implement corrections that make tests pass while preserving intended functionality
4. **Verify understanding:** Request Aider to explain what went wrong and how the fix addresses it

This capability transforms failing tests from frustration points into learning opportunities and rapid debugging sessions.

#### Attaching Results to Commit Messages

Documentation is part of quality. When you commit changes after linting and testing, including results in your commit messages provides valuable context for reviewers and future maintainers.

**Best Practice Commit Message Format:**

[Description of change]

[Context or motivation]

Quality Assurance:
- Linting: All checks passed (ESLint, 0 warnings, 0 errors)
- Testing: 47/47 tests passing
- Coverage: 94% line coverage maintained

Related Issue: #123


This transparency helps teams track code quality trends and provides instant context when investigating historical changes.

#### CI Pipeline Integration

While Aider handles local linting and testing, your CI pipeline provides the authoritative quality gate for all changes. The connection works as follows:

1. **Local Development:** You use Aider with `/lint` and `/test` during development
2. **Commit Trigger:** When you push changes, your CI pipeline activates
3. **Automated Checks:** CI runs identical or additional linting and testing steps
4. **Feedback Loop:** CI results inform your next development session


This two-layer approach (local + CI) ensures issues are caught early and consistently across all contributors.

### Why This Integration Matters for Collaboration

When multiple developers work on the same codebase with AI assistance, consistency becomes challenging. Linting enforces coding standards regardless of who wrote the code or what AI tool assisted. Testing ensures that changes from different contributors integrate smoothly. CI provides a shared quality bar that everyone must meet.

Without this integration, you might get beautifully written but non-functional code, or AI suggestions that seem correct but violate project conventions. With it, you get reliable, consistent, high-quality code delivery.

### Advanced Integration Points

**Pre-commit Hooks:** Configure linters to run automatically before commits, creating frictionless quality enforcement.

**Editor Integration:** Many editors can display linting issues in real-time as you code with Aider.

**Coverage Goals:** Set minimum coverage thresholds in your CI to prevent quality degradation over time.


### The Continuous Improvement Loop

This workflow creates a virtuous cycle: linting catches style issues → testing catches functional issues → AI fixes issues → CI validates fixes → team reviews and merges. Each iteration improves code quality and team efficiency.

## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
// Example 1: Running linters from Aider in a Python project
// This demonstrates the complete linting workflow with Pylint

// Step 1: In your Aider conversation, run the linter:
// /lint pylint

// Step 2: Aider will return results like:
// Running: pylint **/*.py
// Score: 8.5/10
// Issues found: 3 conventions, 1 refactor suggestion

// Step 3: Ask Aider to fix specific issues:
// "Fix all the unused imports identified by the linter"

// Example command structure for common linters:
// /lint pylint --disable=C0114,C0115 ./src/
// /lint flake8 --max-line-length=100 --ignore=E501,W503 .
// /lint mypy --ignore-missing-imports --strict-optional

// Step 4: Verify fixes by running again:
// /lint pylint

// Common workflow with Black for formatting:
// /lint black --check --diff ./src/
// If issues found, ask Aider: "Apply Black formatting to all files"
// Then verify: /lint black --check ./src/
```

### Example 2

```python
# Example 2: Comprehensive test execution and interpretation workflow
# Demonstrates running pytest, interpreting failures, and integrating results

# Step 1: Run your test suite
# /test pytest

# Example output from Aider:
# ================== test session starts ==================
# collected 47 items
# 
# tests/test_api.py::test_get_user FAILED [ 8%]
# tests/test_api.py::test_create_user PASSED [12%]
# tests/test_models.py::test_user_validation PASSED [24%]
# 
# =============== 1 failed, 46 passed in 3.45s ===============

# 
# FAILED: test_get_user - AssertionError: Expected 200, got 404

# Step 2: Ask Aider to diagnose the failure
# "Why did test_get_user fail? Look at the test, the API endpoint, and recent changes"

# Step 3: Aider will analyze and suggest:
# - The route `/users/<id>` might be missing the correct route handler
# - Check if user ID validation is working correctly
# - Verify database queries are executing properly

# Step 4: Ask for the fix
# "Fix the test_get_user failure by correcting the route handler"

# Step 5: Re-run tests to verify
# /test pytest tests/test_api.py::test_get_user

# Step 6: Attach results to commit message
# "Updated user API endpoint handler
#
# Quality Assurance:
# - Linting: ESLint passed (0 errors, 0 warnings)
# - Testing: 47/47 tests passing (including previously failing test)
# - Coverage: Maintained 94% line coverage"
```

### Example 3

```python
# Example 3: CI Pipeline Integration Configuration
# This shows how local Aider testing connects to GitHub Actions CI


# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI Pipeline

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  quality-checks:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
      
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install -r requirements.txt
          pip install pylint pytest pytest-cov
      
      - name: Run linting
        run: |
          echo "Running linting checks..."
          pylint **/*.py --fail-under=9.0
          flake8 . --count --select=E9,F63,F7,F82 --show-source --statistics
      
      - name: Run tests with coverage
        run: |
          echo "Running test suite..."
          pytest --cov=. --cov-report=xml --cov-fail-under=90
      
      - name: Upload coverage to Codecov
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v3
        with:
          files: ./coverage.xml
          flags: unittests
          name: codecov-umbrella

# Local Aider workflow then mirrors CI:
# 1. Developer works with Aider, runs /lint pylint
# 2. Developer runs /test pytest --cov=.
# 3. If local checks pass, commit and push
# 4. CI pipeline runs identical checks as authoritative validation
# 5. CI results inform next development session
```

### Example 4

```python
// Example 4: Multi-language linting workflow
// Demonstrates running different linters for different file types

// JavaScript/TypeScript project:
// /lint eslint --ext .js,.ts --fix
// /lint typescript --project ./tsconfig.json

// Python project:
// /lint pylint ./src/
// /lint mypy ./src/ --ignore-missing-imports

// Combined workflow for full-stack project:
// /lint eslint
// /lint pylint

// After all lints pass, run tests:
// /test pytest
// /test jest

// Full commit readiness check:
// "Run full quality check: ESLint for JS, Pylint for Python, then pytest"
// Aider will sequentially run all checks and summarize results
// Then create a commit message with all results attached
```



## Common Mistakes

- Ignoring linter warnings and only fixing errors, which leads to accumulated technical debt that becomes harder to address later
- Running only a subset of tests instead of the full suite, causing missed regressions that break in CI or production
- Not attaching lint/test results to commit messages, making it difficult to track when quality standards slipped
- Setting overly strict lint rules that cause every file change to fail, leading to developer frustration and workarounds
- Asking Aider to fix linting issues without understanding what changed, creating code that passes linters but loses intended functionality
- Running tests only after all changes are complete instead of iteratively, missing the feedback loop that prevents bugs early
- Assuming CI will catch everything that local testing misses, leading to slow feedback cycles and wasted CI resources
- Not configuring linters consistently between local development and CI, causing discrepancies that are difficult to debug


## Best Practices

- Run linting before every commit using pre-commit hooks to catch issues automatically without relying on manual execution
- Use the same linting and testing configurations locally and in CI to ensure consistency and eliminate 'works on my machine' issues
- Iterate between making changes, running tests, and getting AI feedback rather than making many changes before testing
- Attach comprehensive quality metrics to commit messages following a standardized format that your team can parse and track
- Set realistic but meaningful quality thresholds—pylint scores above 9.0, coverage above 85%—to maintain standards without blocking progress
- When tests fail, ask Aider to diagnose the root cause before implementing fixes to ensure you understand the problem fully
- Integrate coverage reporting into your workflow to identify untested code paths that AI might inadvertently break
- Use linting results as a learning opportunity by asking Aider to explain why specific rules exist, improving your coding skills over time
- Configure editor integrations to show linting issues in real-time, providing instant feedback while you work with Aider
- Regularly review linting and testing trends across your project to identify recurring issues that might indicate architectural problems

# 4. CI pipeline runs identical checks as authoritative validation
# 5. CI results inform next development session
```

### Example 4

```python
// Example 4: Multi-language linting workflow
// Demonstrates running different linters for different file types

// JavaScript/TypeScript project:
// /lint eslint --ext .js,.ts --fix
// /lint typescript --project ./tsconfig.json

// Python project:
// /lint pylint ./src/
// /lint mypy ./src/ --ignore-missing-imports

// Combined workflow for full-stack project:
// /lint eslint
// /lint pylint

// After all lints pass, run tests:
// /test pytest
// /test jest

// Full commit readiness check:
// "Run full quality check: ESLint for JS, Pylint for Python, then pytest"
// Aider will sequentially run all checks and summarize results
// Then create a commit message with all results attached
```



## Common Mistakes

- Ignoring linter warnings and only fixing errors, which leads to accumulated technical debt that becomes harder to address later
- Running only a subset of tests instead of the full suite, causing missed regressions that break in CI or production
- Not attaching lint/test results to commit messages, making it difficult to track when quality standards slipped
- Setting overly strict lint rules that cause every file change to fail, leading to developer frustration and workarounds
- Asking Aider to fix linting issues without understanding what changed, creating code that passes linters but loses intended functionality
- Running tests only after all changes are complete instead of iteratively, missing the feedback loop that prevents bugs early
- Assuming CI will catch everything that local testing misses, leading to slow feedback cycles and wasted CI resources
- Not configuring linters consistently between local development and CI, causing discrepancies that are difficult to debug


## Best Practices

- Run linting before every commit using pre-commit hooks to catch issues automatically without relying on manual execution
- Use the same linting and testing configurations locally and in CI to ensure consistency and eliminate 'works on my machine' issues
- Iterate between making changes, running tests, and getting AI feedback rather than making many changes before testing
- Attach comprehensive quality metrics to commit messages following a standardized format that your team can parse and track
- Set realistic but meaningful quality thresholds—pylint scores above 9.0, coverage above 85%—to maintain standards without blocking progress
- When tests fail, ask Aider to diagnose the root cause before implementing fixes to ensure you understand the problem fully
- Integrate coverage reporting into your workflow to identify untested code paths that AI might inadvertently break
- Use linting results as a learning opportunity by asking Aider to explain why specific rules exist, improving your coding skills over time
- Configure editor integrations to show linting issues in real-time, providing instant feedback while you work with Aider
- Regularly review linting and testing trends across your project to identify recurring issues that might indicate architectural problems
code_examples:
# Example 2: Comprehensive test execution and interpretation workflow
# Demonstrates running pytest, interpreting failures, and integrating results

# Step 1: Run your test suite
# /test pytest

# Example output from Aider:
# ================== test session starts ==================
# collected 47 items
# 
# tests/test_api.py::test_get_user FAILED [ 8%]
# tests/test_api.py::test_create_user PASSED [12%]
# tests/test_models.py::test_user_validation PASSED [24%]
# 
# =============== 1 failed, 46 passed in 3.45s ===============

# 
# FAILED: test_get_user - AssertionError: Expected 200, got 404

# Step 2: Ask Aider to diagnose the failure
# "Why did test_get_user fail? Look at the test, the API endpoint, and recent changes"

# Step 3: Aider will analyze and suggest:
# - The route `/users/<id>` might be missing the correct route handler
# - Check if user ID validation is working correctly
# - Verify database queries are executing properly

# Step 4: Ask for the fix
# "Fix the test_get_user failure by correcting the route handler"

# Step 5: Re-run tests to verify
# /test pytest tests/test_api.py::test_get_user

# Step 6: Attach results to commit message
# "Updated user API endpoint handler
#
# Quality Assurance:
# - Linting: ESLint passed (0 errors, 0 warnings)
# - Testing: 47/47 tests passing (including previously failing test)
# - Coverage: Maintained 94% line coverage"

# Example 3: CI Pipeline Integration Configuration
# This shows how local Aider testing connects to GitHub Actions CI


# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI Pipeline

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  quality-checks:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
      
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install -r requirements.txt
          pip install pylint pytest pytest-cov
      
      - name: Run linting
        run: |
          echo "Running linting checks..."
          pylint **/*.py --fail-under=9.0
          flake8 . --count --select=E9,F63,F7,F82 --show-source --statistics
      
      - name: Run tests with coverage
        run: |
          echo "Running test suite..."
          pytest --cov=. --cov-report=xml --cov-fail-under=90
      
      - name: Upload coverage to Codecov
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v3
        with:
          files: ./coverage.xml
          flags: unittests
          name: codecov-umbrella

# Local Aider workflow then mirrors CI:
# 1. Developer works with Aider, runs /lint pylint
# 2. Developer runs /test pytest --cov=.
# 3. If local checks pass, commit and push
# 4. CI pipeline runs identical checks as authoritative validation
# 5. CI results inform next development session

details.detailed_content:
# Code Review with AI Using Aider

## Concept Explanation: Why AI-Powered Code Review Matters

Traditional code review relies on human reviewers who have limited time, may miss subtle issues, and can have inconsistent feedback quality. AI-powered code review transforms this process by providing instant, consistent, and comprehensive feedback on every code change.

### The Problem with Manual Code Review

- **Bottleneck**: Pull requests sit idle waiting for available reviewers
- **Inconsistency**: Different reviewers have different standards and expertise
- **Surface-level analysis**: Humans often focus on logic and miss style, performance, or security issues
- **Fatigue**: Reviews later in the day or week tend to be less thorough

### How AI Changes the Game

Aider's `/review` command acts as an always-available senior developer who examines your code through multiple lenses: readability, performance optimization, security vulnerabilities, and adherence to best practices. This doesn't replace human reviewers—it augments them by catching issues before human review, allowing reviewers to focus on architecture and logic rather than style violations.

## Core Mechanics: How the `/review` Command Works

### Basic Review Flow

1. **Initiate Review**: You invoke `/review` on a file, diff, or entire commit
2. **AI Analysis**: Aider parses the code and applies multiple analysis dimensions
3. **Structured Feedback**: Comments are generated with severity levels and line references
4. **Actionable Suggestions**: Each issue includes specific refactoring recommendations

### Review Dimensions

| Dimension | What It Checks |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Style** | Formatting, naming conventions, code organization, docstrings |
| **Performance** | Algorithmic complexity, unnecessary computations, memory efficiency |
| **Security** | Input validation, SQL injection, XSS vulnerabilities, secrets exposure |
| **Best Practices** | Design patterns, error handling, testing coverage, dependency management |

### Export and Integration Capabilities

Aider can generate review reports in multiple formats:
- **Markdown reports**: For documentation and historical reference
- **GitHub PR comments**: Direct integration with pull request workflows
- **JSON output**: For programmatic processing and CI/CD pipelines

## Understanding Review Output Structure

When Aider completes a review, it provides structured feedback:


📋 Review Summary
├── Critical Issues: 1
├── Warnings: 3
└── Suggestions: 5

🔴 CRITICAL: user_auth.py:45 - SQL Injection vulnerability
   - Issue: User input directly concatenated into query
   - Fix: Use parameterized queries

🟡 WARNING: api_handler.py:78 - Performance concern
   - Issue: N+1 query pattern detected
   - Fix: Implement eager loading with joins

🟢 SUGGESTION: utils.py:12 - Code style
   - Issue: Function name doesn't follow snake_case
   - Fix: Rename to `calculate_user_age`


## Integration with Development Workflow

### Pre-commit Review
Run AI review before pushing to catch issues early:

bash
aider /review --files src/auth.py --focus security


### Pull Request Integration
Automate reviews on PR creation for consistent feedback:

bash
aider /review --pr --format github -- assignee @senior-dev


### Continuous Review
Integrate into CI/CD for automated checks on every commit:

bash
aider /review --ci --min-grade B --fail-on-critical


## Common Pitfalls and Gotchas

### 1. Over-reliance on AI Suggestions
AI recommendations are suggestions, not gospel. Always validate security-related fixes in context. An AI might suggest "optimizing" a function that's called once at startup—creating complexity for no measurable benefit.

### 2. Ignoring Context
Aider analyzes code in isolation. It cannot know that a "duplicate code" pattern is intentional for API compatibility or that a "security issue" is protected by upstream validation. Always consider the broader context.

### 3. Review Scope Misconfiguration
Running `/review` without scope limits generates overwhelming feedback. Use `--files` or `--diff` to focus on changed code rather than reviewing entire files.

### 4. Expecting Human Judgment
AI reviews excel at pattern matching but struggle with:
- Architectural decisions and trade-offs
- Business logic correctness
- Naming that requires domain knowledge
- Subjective code preferences

### 5. Neglecting to Update Reviews
Code evolves. An AI review from last month may reference line numbers that no longer exist. Re-run reviews after significant changes.

## Practical Advice for Effective AI Code Review

### Setting Up Review Preferences

Create a `.aider.review` configuration file to standardize your review approach:


[review]
default_focus = security,performance
min_grade = B
export_format = markdown
include_context = true
max_issues_per_file = 10


### Maximizing Review Value

1. **Run reviews incrementally**: Review during development, not just before PR submission
2. **Focus iteratively**: Use `--focus` flag to concentrate on specific dimensions
3. **Combine with human review**: Use AI to catch technical issues, humans for design review
4. **Track patterns**: Export reviews to identify recurring issues across your codebase
5. **Customize for your stack**: Configure rules specific to your language and framework

### When to Override AI Suggestions

- **Performance tuning in non-critical paths**: Complexity isn't worth micro-optimizations
- **Style preferences**: Some team conventions may differ from AI defaults
- **Deliberate patterns**: Sometimes the "wrong" approach is correct for your situation
- **Known limitations**: AI may not understand your specific infrastructure constraints

## Advanced Features

### Diff-Based Review
Review only what changed:

bash
aider /review --diff HEAD~1 --focus style,security


### Multi-File Review with Impact Analysis

bash
aider /review --files src/*.py --show-affected-lines


### Comment Threading
For GitHub integration, enable threaded replies:

bash
aider /review --pr --thread-comments --collapse-unchanged


## Summary

AI-powered code review with Aider transforms how teams maintain code quality. By providing instant, consistent feedback across style, performance, and security dimensions, it supplements human expertise without replacing it. The key to success lies in understanding its strengths—pattern detection and comprehensive coverage—while acknowledging its limitations in context-dependent judgment. Use it as a first-pass filter that surfaces issues, then apply human wisdom to decide which suggestions to implement.


## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
# Example 1: Basic File Review with Security Focus
# Run a targeted review on authentication-related code

aider /review --files auth.py --focus security,style --min-grade B

# Expected output structure:
# 📋 Review Summary for auth.py
# ├── Security Issues: 2
# ├── Style Issues: 5
# └── Suggestions: 8

# After review completes, export results:
/review --export review_report.md --format markdown

# The generated report includes:
# - Line-specific comments with severity levels
# - Code snippets showing problematic patterns
# - Suggested fixes with before/after examples
# - Overall grade and pass/fail status
```

### Example 2

```python
# Example 2: Pull Request Review with GitHub Integration
# This example shows a complete PR review workflow

# Step 1: Review a specific diff against main branch
aider /review \
  --diff main..feature/user-auth \
  --focus performance,security \
  --format json \
  --output pr_review.json

# Step 2: Post review comments to GitHub PR
aider /review \
  --pr 123 \
  --format github-comment \
  --reviewer @code-review-team \
  --collapse-unchanged-files

# Step 3: Generate markdown summary for PR description
aider /review \
  --pr 123 \
  --export pr_summary.md \
  --include-summary-table \
  --diff main..feature/user-auth

# Step 4: CI/CD integration with pass/fail criteria
# Exit code 0 if review passes, 1 if critical issues found
aider /review \
  --diff HEAD~1 \
  --min-grade B \
  --fail-on-critical \
  --format json > review_result.json

# The JSON output can be parsed by CI tools:
# {
#   "grade": "B",
#   "passed": true,
#   "critical_issues": 0,
#   "warnings": 3,
#   "suggestions": 12
# }
```

### Example 3

```python
# Example 3: Selective Review with Context
# Review specific functions rather than entire files

aider /review \
  --files database.py \
  --focus performance \
  --lines 45-67 \
  --include-context 3

# This shows:
# - Review of lines 45-67 (targeted function)
# - 3 lines of context before and after
# - Performance-specific analysis

# Example 4: Batch Review with Config File
# Use configuration for consistent team reviews

# Create .aider.review in project root:
# [review]
# focus = security,style
# min_grade = B
# export_format = markdown
# max_issues = 50

# Then run unified team review
aider /review \
  --files src/**/*.py \
  --config .aider.review \
  --exclude tests/*,migrations/*

# Example 5: Iterative Review Workflow
# Combine multiple review commands for comprehensive analysis

aider

# First pass: Quick style check
/review --files new_feature.py --focus style

# Address issues, then deep security analysis
/review --files new_feature.py --focus security --verbose

# Finally, performance validation
/review --files new_feature.py --focus performance --suggest-alternatives

# Export final comprehensive report
/review --export final_review.md --include-all-passed-checks
```



## Common Mistakes

- Running full codebase reviews instead of targeting changed files, which produces overwhelming output and masks relevant issues
- Accepting all AI suggestions without evaluating whether they improve code in your specific context
- Ignoring the --focus flag and getting reviews across all dimensions when you only need security or style feedback
- Not re-running reviews after code changes, leading to stale line number references in feedback
- Using AI review as the sole quality gate instead of combining it with human expert review
- Failing to configure minimum grade thresholds, resulting in too many low-priority suggestions cluttering output
- Not exporting reviews to markdown for future reference, losing valuable historical feedback
- Expecting AI to understand business logic or domain-specific constraints that require human context


## Best Practices

- Run targeted reviews using --files and --diff flags to focus on actual changes rather than reviewing entire files
- Use the --focus flag to concentrate on specific dimensions (security, performance, style) based on what matters most for your current work
- Configure a .aider.review file with team-specific standards including minimum grade thresholds and preferred conventions
- Integrate AI review into your pre-commit workflow to catch issues before code reaches pull requests
- Export reviews to markdown for documentation and track recurring issues across your codebase over time
- Combine AI review with human review: let AI handle style and pattern issues, humans focus on architecture and business logic
- Re-run reviews after significant code changes to ensure feedback remains accurate and line references are current
- Use --include-context appropriately to give AI enough surrounding code to understand context without overwhelming it
- Set up CI/CD integration with --fail-on-critical to enforce minimum quality standards automatically
- Review and iterate on your review configuration regularly as your codebase and team standards evolve

- Integrate AI review into your pre-commit workflow to catch issues before code reaches pull requests
- Export reviews to markdown for documentation and track recurring issues across your codebase over time
- Combine AI review with human review: let AI handle style and pattern issues, humans focus on architecture and business logic
- Re-run reviews after significant code changes to ensure feedback remains accurate and line references are current
- Use --include-context appropriately to give AI enough surrounding code to understand context without overwhelming it
- Set up CI/CD integration with --fail-on-critical to enforce minimum quality standards automatically
- Review and iterate on your review configuration regularly as your codebase and team standards evolve
code_examples:
# Example 1: Basic File Review with Security Focus
# Run a targeted review on authentication-related code

aider /review --files auth.py --focus security,style --min-grade B

# Expected output structure:
# 📋 Review Summary for auth.py
# ├── Security Issues: 2
# ├── Style Issues: 5
# └── Suggestions: 8

# After review completes, export results:
/review --export review_report.md --format markdown

# The generated report includes:
# - Line-specific comments with severity levels
# - Code snippets showing problematic patterns
# - Suggested fixes with before/after examples
# - Overall grade and pass/fail status

# Example 2: Pull Request Review with GitHub Integration
# This example shows a complete PR review workflow

# Step 1: Review a specific diff against main branch
aider /review \
  --diff main..feature/user-auth \
  --focus performance,security \
  --format json \
  --output pr_review.json

# Step 2: Post review comments to GitHub PR
aider /review \
  --pr 123 \
  --format github-comment \
  --reviewer @code-review-team \
  --collapse-unchanged-files

# Step 3: Generate markdown summary for PR description
aider /review \
  --pr 123 \
  --export pr_summary.md \
  --include-summary-table \
  --diff main..feature/user-auth

# Step 4: CI/CD integration with pass/fail criteria
# Exit code 0 if review passes, 1 if critical issues found
aider /review \
  --diff HEAD~1 \
  --min-grade B \
  --fail-on-critical \
  --format json > review_result.json

# The JSON output can be parsed by CI tools:
# {
#   "grade": "B",
#   "passed": true,
#   "critical_issues": 0,
#   "warnings": 3,
#   "suggestions": 12
# }

# Example 3: Selective Review with Context
# Review specific functions rather than entire files

aider /review \
  --files database.py \
  --focus performance \
  --lines 45-67 \
  --include-context 3

# This shows:
# - Review of lines 45-67 (targeted function)
# - 3 lines of context before and after
# - Performance-specific analysis

# Example 4: Batch Review with Config File
# Use configuration for consistent team reviews

# Create .aider.review in project root:
# [review]
# focus = security,style
# min_grade = B
# export_format = markdown
# max_issues = 50

# Then run unified team review
aider /review \
  --files src/**/*.py \
  --config .aider.review \
  --exclude tests/*,migrations/*

# Example 5: Iterative Review Workflow
# Combine multiple review commands for comprehensive analysis

aider

# First pass: Quick style check
/review --files new_feature.py --focus style

# Address issues, then deep security analysis
/review --files new_feature.py --focus security --verbose

# Finally, performance validation
/review --files new_feature.py --focus performance --suggest-alternatives

# Export final comprehensive report
/review --export final_review.md --include-all-passed-checks

sub_nodes.title: Custom Prompt Patterns and AGENTS.md
sub_nodes.description: Create configuration files to inject domain knowledge, style guides, and reusable instructions.

details.detailed_content:
## Understanding AGENTS.md: Your Project's Instruction Manual

### Concept Explanation

AGENTS.md is a special configuration file that serves as a persistent instruction layer for Aider. Unlike chat-based instructions that are forgotten between sessions, AGENTS.md lives in your project root and automatically loads every time you work with that codebase. Think of it as writing a detailed brief for a new team member—AGENTS.md communicates your project's conventions, standards, and expectations to the AI assistant consistently across all coding sessions.

### Why This Matters

When you're working on a large project, you often repeat the same instructions: coding style preferences, architectural decisions, testing requirements, or domain-specific terminology. Without AGENTS.md, you'd need to re-explain these context details every session, leading to inconsistent results and wasted time. AGENTS.md solves this by establishing a single source of truth that the AI references automatically, ensuring your assistant always understands your project's unique context and standards.

### Core Mechanics: How AGENTS.md Works

**File Location and Loading**: Aider looks for AGENTS.md in the repository root upon initialization. The file is automatically read and its contents are prepended to every prompt sent to the language model. This happens silently in the background—you don't need to reference it explicitly.

**Content Structure**: The file accepts free-form Markdown, giving you flexibility to structure instructions as you see fit. However, for optimal results, organize content into clear sections: project overview, coding conventions, preferred patterns, testing requirements, and environment specifics.

**Prompt Block Integration**: You can embed reusable prompt blocks that define complex instructions. These blocks become part of the base context, allowing you to reference architectural patterns, testing strategies, or specific conventions without repeating yourself.

**Environment-Specific Context**: AGENTS.md supports conditional logic through special syntax. You can define context that applies only to specific environments, branches, or conditions, making the file adaptable to different development scenarios.

### The Build Process

Creating an effective AGENTS.md involves three phases. First, you establish baseline knowledge by documenting project structure, key technologies, and architectural decisions. Second, you encode conventions covering coding style, naming patterns, testing requirements, and documentation standards. Third, you layer in specialized instructions for specific domains or workflows unique to your project.

### Version Control Integration

AGENTS.md should be committed to version control alongside your code. This ensures consistency across team members and across different machines. When a new developer clones the repository, they automatically receive the same context and instructions you defined. Treat AGENTS.md as documentation—it evolves with your project and should be reviewed and updated as your codebase changes.

### Relationship with Command-Line Instructions

AGENTS.md complements rather than replaces command-line instructions. You can still pass temporary context via the `--read` flag or inline prompts. AGENTS.md provides the persistent foundation, while direct instructions handle session-specific needs. This layering creates a comprehensive instruction system where base knowledge persists and ephemeral context can be added as needed.


## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
# AGENTS.md - Example for a Node.js REST API Project
# Place this file in your repository root

## Project Overview
Our codebase implements a microservice-based REST API using Node.js and Express.
Framework version: Express 4.x | Node version: 18+
Database: PostgreSQL with Prisma ORM

## Coding Conventions

### Style Guidelines
- Use 2-space indentation, no tabs
- Prefer const over let; avoid var
- Always use async/await over callbacks or .then() chains
- Maximum function length: 30 lines; break larger functions into helpers

### Naming Standards
- Files: kebab-case (e.g., user-auth.controller.js)
- Functions: camelCase (e.g., createUserSession)
- Classes/Components: PascalCase (e.g., UserController)
- Constants: SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE (e.g., MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS)
- Database tables: snake_case plural (e.g., user_sessions)

## Testing Requirements
- Every function requires unit tests with minimum 80% coverage
- Integration tests required for all API endpoints
- Use Jest framework; describe blocks should match the module name
- Mock all external service calls (database, APIs)
- Include meaningful test descriptions: "should return 401 when token is invalid"

## Architectural Patterns

### Request-Response Flow
1. Controller receives request, validates input
2. Service layer handles business logic
3. Repository handles data access through Prisma
4. Controller formats and returns response

### Error Handling
- Use custom error classes extending BaseError
- Always include error codes and user-friendly messages
- Log errors with context (request ID, user ID) for debugging
- Never expose internal stack traces to clients

## Domain-Specific Context

### Authentication Flow
- JWT tokens with 15-minute expiry stored in httpOnly cookies
- Refresh tokens valid for 7 days, stored in database
- Always validate tokens on every request using middleware

### Rate Limiting
- Public endpoints: 100 requests per minute per IP
- Authenticated endpoints: 1000 requests per minute per user
- Use Redis for distributed rate limiting across instances

```

### Example 2

```python
# Python Data Processing Pipeline - AGENTS.md Example
# Example for a data engineering project

## Project Purpose
Data pipeline for processing customer analytics events from Kafka to BigQuery.
Handles approximately 2 million events daily with real-time processing requirements.

## Data Conventions

### Schema Standards
- All timestamps in UTC, formatted as ISO 8601
- Required fields: event_id (UUID), timestamp, event_type, payload
- Optional metadata fields prefixed with underscore: _source_system, _processing_version
- No null values in final tables; use appropriate defaults or -1 for unknown

### Naming Patterns
- Tables: {environment}_{domain}_{entity} (e.g., prod_analytics_page_views)
- Columns: snake_case descriptive names
- Datasets/Schemas: lowercase with underscores
- Environment labels: dev, staging, prod (never 'production')

## Pipeline Patterns

### Transformation Rules
1. Always include idempotency keys to prevent duplicate processing
2. Log processing time for each transformation stage
3. Use schema validation libraries (Pydantic) before writing to output
4. Implement dead-letter queues for failed records with full error context

### Monitoring Requirements
- Emit metrics for: records processed, records failed, processing latency
- Use consistent metric naming: {pipeline_name}_{stage}_{metric_type}
- Alert thresholds: failure rate > 1%, latency p99 > 5 seconds

## Code Organization
- Airflow DAGs in /dags directory
- Transform functions in /transforms directory
- Shared utilities in /lib directory
- Tests mirror source structure in /tests directory

## Environment-Specific Context
<!-- @env staging -->
In staging, use test Kafka topics with 'staging_' prefix.
All BigQuery writes go to development datasets with '_dev' suffix.

<!-- @env production -->
Production uses primary Kafka clusters with exactly-once semantics.
All writes go to production datasets; no suffix.
Enable additional validation and logging for debugging.

```

### Example 3

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Demonstration: How to dynamically read and use AGENTS.md context
This script shows how you might programmatically work with agent instructions.
"""

import os
from pathlib import Path

def load_agents_md_content(repo_path: str) -> str | None:
    """Load AGENTS.md content from repository root."""
    agents_file = Path(repo_path) / "AGENTS.md"
    if agents_file.exists():
        with open(agents_file, 'r') as f:
            return f.read()
    return None

def extract_sections(content: str) -> dict[str, str]:
    """
    Parse AGENTS.md into sections based on ## headings.
    Useful for conditional processing or tool-specific extraction.
    """
    sections = {}
    current_section = "header"
    current_content = []
    
    for line in content.split('\n'):
        if line.startswith('## '):
            if current_content:
                sections[current_section] = '\n'.join(current_content)
            current_section = line[3:].strip()
            current_content = []
        else:
            current_content.append(line)
    
    if current_content:
        sections[current_section] = '\n'.join(current_content)
    return sections

def extract_environment_blocks(content: str) -> dict[str, str]:
    """
    Extract environment-specific blocks marked with <!-- @env -->
    These enable context-sensitive instruction loading.
    """
    blocks = {}
    current_env = None
    current_content = []
    
    for line in content.split('\n'):
        if line.strip().startswith('<!-- @env '):
            if current_env and current_content:
                blocks[current_env] = '\n'.join(current_content)
            current_env = line.split('@env')[1].split('-->')[0].strip()
            current_content = []
        elif current_env is not None:
            if not line.strip().startswith('-->'):
                current_content.append(line)
    
    if current_env and current_content:
        blocks[current_env] = '\n'.join(current_content)
    
    return blocks

# Example usage in a workflow
if __name__ == "__main__":
    repo_path = "/path/to/your/project"
    content = load_agents_md_content(repo_path)
    
    if content:
        print("Found AGENTS.md!")
        print(f"Total length: {len(content)} characters")
        
        sections = extract_sections(content)
        print(f"\nSections found: {list(sections.keys())}")
        
        env_blocks = extract_environment_blocks(content)
        print(f"Environment blocks: {list(env_blocks.keys())}")
    else:
        print("No AGENTS.md found in repository root")

```



## Common Mistakes

- Including instructions that are too generic or obvious—AGENTS.md should contain project-specific knowledge, not basic coding advice like 'use meaningful variable names'
- Making AGENTS.md too verbose with hundreds of rules—the file becomes unmanageable and the AI ignores overly long contexts; aim for focused, essential information
- Forgetting to update AGENTS.md when project conventions change—stale instructions lead to code that doesn't match current standards
- Using AGENTS.md as a dumping ground for all project documentation—keep it focused on instructions for AI interaction, separate comprehensive docs to other files
- Not version controlling AGENTS.md—if you modify it locally without committing, team members get inconsistent behavior
- Including sensitive information like API keys, passwords, or internal security details in plain text—AGENTS.md may be shared or stored in ways that expose secrets
- Overloading AGENTS.md with examples instead of patterns—provide general rules that apply broadly rather than exhaustive examples for every scenario


## Best Practices

- Start with a minimal AGENTS.md focused on your project's unique conventions—add more context gradually as you discover needs rather than creating an exhaustive file upfront
- Organize content into clear sections with headers—use Markdown formatting to create scannable structure that both humans and AI can parse effectively
- Review and update AGENTS.md during code reviews when you notice the AI making consistent mistakes or missing project-specific context
- Keep AGENTS.md focused on actionable instructions rather than background information—include 'why' context only when it affects how instructions should be applied
- Use environment-specific blocks (<!-- @env -->) for context that varies between development, staging, and production—this keeps main content environment-agnostic
- Commit AGENTS.md changes alongside the code they relate to—when adding a new service, update instructions in the same PR where the service is introduced
- Test your AGENTS.md effectiveness by asking the AI about project conventions and verifying it reflects your documented standards accurately
